timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ark Brought to Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — The Ark Placed; Offerings and Blessings

refs.note: The parallel in Samuel is brief; the Chronicler expands the liturgical details.

David's thanksgiving hymn in vv.23-33 closely parallels Psalm 96 — the Chronicler either quotes the psalm or both draw from a common liturgical tradition, demonstrating the worship life that accompanied the ark.
The opening section of David's hymn (vv.8-22) corresponds to the first fifteen verses of Psalm 105 — a celebration of God's covenant faithfulness from Abraham through the patriarchal period.

notes.note: Daily worship teaches that God deserves not occasional attention but constant devotion. The assignment of regular ministers ensures that praise does not depend on the king’s mood but on institutional commitment. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The three verbs — lĕhazkir (to invoke/remember), lĕhōdōt (to give thanks), lĕhallēl (to praise) — define the three modes of Levitical worship ministry. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The ark is set inside the tent David pitched for it. Burnt offerings and fellowship offerings are made. David blesses the people in the name of the LORD and distributes food to “each Israelite man and woman.” Then David assigns Asaph and his associates to minister regularly before the ark “according to each day’s requirements.” Worship is not a one-time event but an ongoing institution.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew qōḏeš (holiness) denotes separation for divine purpose. In the context of temple worship, it marks the distinction between common and sacred — a boundary that defines Israel's entire liturgical life. The Chronicler's emphasis on holiness reflects his concern for proper worship as the foundation of national life.

sections.header: Verses 8–43 — The Psalm; Regular Worship at Gibeon and Jerusalem

heb.paragraph: The psalm includes the command: “Look to the LORD and his strength; seek his face always” (v.11). The verb darash (“seek”) is the Chronicler’s central theological term, and here it appears in David’s own psalm. The king commands what the Chronicler preaches: seek God.

refs.note: The first section of David’s psalm, celebrating God’s covenant and mighty deeds.
The second section, calling all nations to worship the LORD.
The closing doxology: “Save us, LORD our God, and gather us from the nations.”

notes.note: The call for “all the earth” to sing to the LORD teaches that God’s worship was never intended to be confined to Israel. The missionary impulse is embedded in David’s first psalm. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The plea “gather us and deliver us from the nations” echoes Ps 106:47 but takes on new urgency in the Chronicler’s post-exilic context. The gathered community prays for further gathering. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David’s psalm is a composite: vv.8–22 = Psalm 105:1–15; vv.23–33 = Psalm 96:1–13; vv.34–36 = Psalm 106:1, 47–48. The Chronicler attributes this liturgical anthology to David and places it at the founding moment of Jerusalemite worship. The psalm covers God’s covenant faithfulness, his universal kingship, and a plea for deliverance. After the psalm, the Chronicler notes the dual worship arrangement: Asaph’s group at the ark in Jerusalem; Zadok’s group at the tabernacle in Gibeon. Two sites, one worship.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAsaph was the chief, and next to him in rank were Zechariah, then Jaaziel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Mattithiah, Eliab, Benaiah, Obed-Edom and Jeiel. They were to play the lyres and harps, Asaph was to sound the cymbals,ESVAsaph was the chief, and second to him were Zechariah, Jeiel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Mattithiah, Eliab, Benaiah, Obed-edom, and Jeiel, who were to play harps and lyres; Asaph was to sound the cymbals,NIVThen he gave a loaf of bread, a cake of dates and a cake of raisins to each Israelite man and woman.ESVand distributed to all Israel, both men and women, to each a loaf of bread, a portion of meat, and a cake of raisins.
